content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Mr. Quinn M. Barrow 
Richards Watson Gershon 
355 South Grand Avenue, 40th Floor 
Los Angeles, CA 90071-31 01 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-16-063 
Dear Mr. Barrow: 
April 15, 2016 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Manhattan Beach City 
Councilmember Tony D' Errico regarding conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act 
(the "Act").1 This letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission 
does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.) 
QUESTION 
Does the Act prohibit Councilmember D'Errico from taking part in the governmental 
decision on whether to approve the Downtown Specific Plan ("DSP")? 
CONCLUSION 
Yes. The Act prohibits the Councilmember from taking part in the decision on whether to 
approve the DSP because the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect 
on the Councilmember's financial interests in businesses within the area of the DSP. 
FACTS 
You are the City Attorney for the City of Manhattan Beach and the authorized 
representative of City Councilmember Tony D'Errico. The City will soon deliberate on the contents 
of, and consider whether to approve, the DSP. 
The Draft DSP.sets forth the following "Vision Goals" for the DSP: (I) preserve and 
enhance the DSP area's small town character; (2) enhance pedestrian and bicycle access; (3) 
improve access to parking and alternative transportation options; and ( 4) encourage economic 
development. In order to achieve these goals, the DSP wilt update the City's Zoning Code, 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-16-063 
Page No. 2 
Downtown Parking Management Plan, Draft Mobility Plan, Community Facilities Strategic Plan, 
and Downtown Design Guidelines. 
With respect to the development potential of the DSP area, the Draft DSP identifies 
opportunities to provide parking solutions, streetscape improvements, and enhanced pedestrian and 
bicycle facilities, and to retain and strengthen existing businesses. The Draft DSP's proposed 
changes in commercial development standards within the DSP area include the establishment of 
maximum setbacks, optional upper floor stepbacks, a faėade transparency standard, and a maximum 
amount of frontage for an individual building along a street. 
The Draft DSP's revisions to the Circulation Plan would include the addition of pedestrian 
plazas, enhanced crosswalks, and multi-use drop-off zones. The Draft DSP's updates to the 
Downtown Parking Management Plan would include demand meter pricing, smart parking 
technologies, a new underground parking structure, remote parking lots and shuttles, and an intra­
downtown resident parking system, and other creative methods to alleviate parking shortages. 
The Draft DSP's update to the Downtown Design Guidelines includes public realm design 
improvements, including the addition of modem street furnishings, street landscaping, improved 
lighting, midblock crosswalks, welcome signage, public art, and bike racks. 
The Draft DSP would also establish goals and strategies for advancing economic 
development within the DSP area, including implementation of a business strategy focused on 
retention and expansion of existing businesses. 
The Councilmember and his wife jointly own and lease two properties located roughly in 
the center of the DSP area, and they operate separate retail clothing stores at each property. 
Although the stores have different addresses, they are located in a single building. Monthly rent for 
each property is in excess of$4,200, and subject to an escalator based on the Consumer Price Index. 
The leases for both properties have been extended through April 30, 2017, and the D'Erricos have 
the option to extend the leases for at least one additional three-year term. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise 
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a 
financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a decision, within the meaning of the 
Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one or 
more of the official's interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) 
Section 87103 sets forth the financial interests from which a conflict of interest may arise 
under the Act, and of those interests, the decision on whether to approve the DSP and the facts 
presented implicate the following interests of the Council member: 


File No. A-16-063 
Page No. 3 
Retail Clothing Stores: Because the Councilmember is a joint owner and operator of two 
retail clothing stores, each with monthly rent expenses in excess of $4,200, he has a financial 
interest in each of the stores as a business entity in which he has directly or indirectly invested 
$2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a}.} He also has an interest in each of the stores as a business entity 
in which he holds a management position (Section 87103(d}}. Assuming that the Councilmember 
receives income from either business of $500 or more in the 12 months prior to the decision, he also 
has an interest in that business as a source ofincome. (Section 87103(c).) 
Customers of Retail Clothing Stores: If a public official owns a I 0-percent or greater 
interest in a business, customers who are sources of income to that business are also sources of 
income to the official. (Section 82030(a).) Here, the Councilmember and his wife are joint owners 
of the retail clothing stores. Therefore, the Councilmember has a financial interest in any customer 
of the stores if the income derived from that customer aggregates to $500 or more in the 12 months 
prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).} 
Store Leases: Because the Councilmember and his wife lease the real property parcels 
underlying the retail clothing stores, and because he has an investment in each of those parcels 
worth $2,000 or more, evidenced by the monthly rent in excess of $4,200 on each parcel, he has a 
financial interest in each of those parcels. (Section 87103{b}.) 
Personal Financial Effect: A public official always has a financial interest in his or her 
personal finances or the personal finances of his immediate family. (Regulation 18702.5.) 
Thus, the Councilmember has financial interests in the retail clothing stores as business 
entities and as sources of income, in customers of the stores as sources of income, in the parcels of 
real property underlying the stores, and in his personal finances and the personal finances of his 
immediate family. 
Foreseeability 
In order to determine if the Councilmember has a conflict of interest under the Act with respect 
to the decision on whether to approve the DSP, we must consider whether a financial effect of the 
decision on one or more of the Councilmember's financial interests would be reasonably foreseeable. 
Under the Act, an effect on an interest is presumed foreseeable if the interest is explicitly involved in the 
decision, and an interest is "explicitly involved" if the interest is a named party in, or subject of, the 
decision. (Regulation l 870l(a).) In this case, the Councilmember's interests are not explicitly involved 
in the decision on whether to approve the DSP. Therefore, an effect of the decision on one or more of 
the Councilmember' s interests is reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility 
and more than hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation 18701 (b ). ) 
The Draft DSP's encouragement of economic development, identification of opportunities to 
retain and strengthen existing businesses, and potential provision of improvements within the DSP area 
could have a beneficial financial effect on the Councilmember's retail clothing businesses located 
roughly in the center of the DSP area. Thus, the effect of the decision on whether to approve the DSP 
will have a reasonably foreseeable effect on the Councilmember' s financial interests in those businesses. 


Materiality 
File No. A-16-063 
Page No. 4 
Regulation 18702.1 sets forth the materiality standards applicable to a decision's financial 
effect on a public official's financial interest in a business entity, including a business entity that is 
a source of income. Regulation 18702.1 (b) provides that the effect of the decision is material if a 
prudent person with sufficient information would find that the effect would contribute to a change 
in the value of a privately-held business entity. Regulation 18702. l (b) also enumerates examples of 
the types of decisions that may meet the materiality standard provided therein, and the fourth 
example describes a decision that would "[m]ake improvements in the surrounding neighborhood 
such as redevelopment projects, traffic/road improvements, or parking changes that may affect, 
either temporarily or permanently, the amount of business the business entity receives." (Regulation 
18702.1 (b)(4).) 
The decision on whether to approve the DSP may result in improvements in the 
neighborhood surrounding the DSP area, traffic/road improvements, and parking changes that may 
affect the amount of business that the Councilmember's retail clothing stores, located roughly in the 
center of the DSP area, receive. Moreover, one of the primary purposes of the DSP is to encourage 
economic development, and in the portion of the Draft DSP dedicated to economic development, 
the first enumerated goal is to "(i]mplement business strategy focused on retention/expansion." 
Therefore, a reasonably prudent person with sufficient information would find that the financial 
effect of the decision would contribute to a change in the value of Councilmember's stores. 
Thus, we conclude that the Act prohibits the Councilmember from taking part in the 
decision on whether to approve the DSP because the decision would have a reasonably foreseeable 
material financial effect on the Councilmember's financial interest in his retail clothing stores 
within the area of the DSP.2 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
MFC:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
By: 
Matthew F. Christy 
Counsel, Legal Division 
2 We do not analyze the foreseeability or materiality of the Councilmember's other financial interests in the 
decision under the Act because there is a clear conflict of interest resulting from his interest in the retail clothing 
businesses as business entities and sources of income.

Does the Act prohibit Councilmember D'Errico from taking part in the governmental 
decision on whether to approve the Downtown Specific Plan ("DSP")?

approve the DSP because the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect 
on the Councilmember's financial interests in businesses within the area of the DSP.

Section 87100 prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise 
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a 
financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a decision, within the meaning of the

Section 87103 sets forth the financial interests from which a conflict of interest may arise 
under the Act, and of those interests, the decision on whether to approve the DSP and the facts

Retail Clothing Stores: Because the Councilmember is a joint owner and operator of two

$2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a}.} He also has an interest in each of the stores as a business entity 
in which he holds a management position (Section 87103(d}}. Assuming that the Councilmember 
receives income from either business of $500 or more in the 12 months prior to the decision, he also 
has an interest in that business as a source ofincome. (Section 87103(c).)

interest in a business, customers who are sources of income to that business are also sources of

of the retail clothing stores. Therefore, the Councilmember has a financial interest in any customer

entities and as sources of income, in customers of the stores as sources of income, in the parcels of 
real property underlying the stores, and in his personal finances and the personal finances of his 
immediate family.

Under the Act, an effect on an interest is presumed foreseeable if the interest is explicitly involved in the

Regulation 18702.1 sets forth the materiality standards applicable to a decision's financial

a source of income. Regulation 18702.1 (b) provides that the effect of the decision is material if a

in the value of a privately-held business entity. Regulation 18702. l (b) also enumerates examples of

example describes a decision that would "[m]ake improvements in the surrounding neighborhood

The decision on whether to approve the DSP may result in improvements in the

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does the Act prohibit Councilmember D'Errico from taking part in the governmental 
decision on whether to approve the Downtown Specific Plan ("DSP")?

CONCLUSION: Yes. The Act prohibits the Councilmember from taking part in the decision on whether to 
approve the DSP because the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect 
on the Councilmember's financial interests in businesses within the area of the DSP.
